segments.text: Nixutah 6.2.19
Once, when the Exalted One was staying in the brick hall at Nadiqah,
He addressed the monk saying,
Munks, Lord they replied,
and the Exalted One said,
Munks, mindfulness of death, when developed,
made to increase, is very fruitful,
great in wheel, merging in the deathless,
having the deathless as consummation.
Munks, developed mindfulness of death more.
And when he had spoken thus, a monk said to him,
I, Lord, develop mindfulness of death.
How so, Munks?
Hearing Lord, such is my thought,
were I day and night to abide mindful of the Exalted One's word.
Much would be done by me.
Thus, Lord, I develop mindfulness of death.
And another said, I too, Lord, develop mindfulness of death.
How so, Munks?
Hearing Lord, such is my thought,
were I day long to abide mindful of the Exalted One's word.
Much would be done by me.
And another said, such is my thought,
were I to abide mindful just so long as I eat an arm's meal.
And another said, were I to abide mindful
as I munch and swallow four or five morsels of food.
And another said, as I munch and swallow only one morsel of food.
And another said, I too, Lord, develop mindfulness of death.
How so, Munks?
Lord, such is my thought, were I to abide
just so long as I breathe in and out,
or breathe out and in, mindful of the Exalted One's word.
Much would be done by me.
Thus, Lord, I develop mindfulness of death.
And when he has spoken thus, the Exalted One said to the Munks,
Munks, the Munks who makes mindfulness of death,
who develops mindfulness of death thus,
were I day and night to abide mindful of the Exalted One's word.
Or he who thinks thus, were I day long to abide mindful of the Exalted One's word.
Or the one who thinks, as I eat an arm's meal,
or as I munch and swallow four or five morsels of food,
mindful of the Exalted's words, much would be done by me.
Those Munks are said to live indolently.
Slightly they develop mindfulness of death
for the destruction of the Asabas.
But the Munk who makes mindfulness,
who develops mindfulness of death thus,
were I to abide mindful of the Exalted One's word,
as I munch and swallow one morsel of food.
And he who thinks thus, were I to abide mindful of the Exalted One's word,
as I breathe in and out, or out and in, much would be done by me.
These Munks are said to live earnestly.
Keenly they develop mindfulness of death
for the destruction of the Asabas.
Therefore, Munks, train yourselves thus.
We will live earnestly.
Keenly will we develop mindfulness of death
for the destruction of the Asabas.
Train yourselves thus, Munks.
That's the end of the Sutta.
This mindfulness of death is a very important practice
for us to, in order that we may turn away from the world
and practice the holy life.
If we were not to remind ourselves
that this body is going to die one day
and that one day is not very far away,
then we would still be very interested in the world
because there are many things in the world,
especially nowadays, that would attract us.
But it is only because we keep reminding ourselves
that death would come in the not-too-distant time
that we have this earnestness, this incentive
to practice the holy life.
You notice the Buddha said about the developed,
were I to abide mindful of the Exalted One's word,
much would be done by me.
This mindful of the Buddha's word,
that is mindfulness of the Dhamma, Dhammanusati,
one of the objects of the Satipatana meditation.
In this Sutta, we can see that this Dhammanusati
is the Satipatana Sutta.
Some people translate it as contemplating the thoughts,
but here it would seem to be,
Dhamma is the Buddha's words, contemplating the Buddha's words
because by contemplating the Buddha's words,
the discourses of the Buddha,
that is one way to become enlightened or become an Arya.
I mentioned before in one of the Suttas,
in the Chapter of the Fives, the Buddha said
there are five times a person can become an Arya,
one is listening to Dhamma, another one is teaching Dhamma,
another one is repeating Dhamma,
another one is reflecting on Dhamma,
and then the fifth one is during meditation.
So in this Sutta, the Buddha said,
if we only reflect on death,
then in a way that you ought to reflect on the Buddha's words
only once a day or once a day and night,
then the Buddha said that is being very slack,
that is not being earnest about the practice,
but one who develops mindfulness of death
so that with every breath,
he reminds himself of death,
or with every mouthful of food,
he reminds himself of death,
and he thinks of the Buddha's words.
Then such a person is said to be earnest.
In other words, the Buddha is trying to tell us
that as often as possible,
we should remember to practice mindfulness of death
as often as we can,
we should contemplate on the Buddha's words.
The next Sutta is 6.3.27.
Now a certain monk visited the exalted one,
saluted him and sat down at one side,
and so seated he said to the exalted one,
Lord, how often should one go and see a monk
who has developed mind?
Mano Bhavaniya,
how often should one go and see a monk who has developed mind?
And the Buddha said,
There are these six times
when one should go and see a monk who has developed mind.
What six?
Here in monk,
when a monk is obsessed in heart by passionate lust,
overwhelmed by passionate lust,
and knows no refuge,
as there really is from the search of passionate lust,
then let him visit a monk who has developed mind,
and say this to him,
Good sir, I am indeed obsessed in heart by passionate lust,
overwhelmed thereby,
nor know I any refuge from the search thereof.
Well worried for me if the venerable one would teach me Dhamma
to get rid of this lust thing,
then the monk would teach him Dhamma
to rid him of that lust.
This monk is the first time to go and see a monk
who has developed mind.
Again,
when in heart he is obsessed by ill will,
or sloth and torpor,
or restlessness and worry,
or doubt,
is overwhelmed thereby,
nor knows a refuge from the search thereof.
Then let him visit a monk who has developed mind,
and say,
Good sir, I am obsessed in heart by this and that,
nor know I any refuge from the search thereof.
Let the venerable one teach me Dhamma,
and he does so.
This monk is the second, third, fourth, and fifth time
to go and see a monk who has developed mind.
Moreover, monk,
when from some object as he concentrates thereon,
there comes not to him at intervals as a destruction,
nor knows him,
nor knows he that object nor realizes it,
then let him visit a monk who has developed mind,
and say,
Good sir, from this object as I concentrate thereon,
there comes not to me at intervals as a destruction,
nor know I this object nor realize it,
well word for me if the venerable one would teach me Dhamma
to the end that I may destroy the asavas,
then the monk will teach him Dhamma to destroy the asavas.
This monk is the sixth time to go and see a monk
who has developed mind.
That's the end of the Sutta.
Here the Buddha is saying,
Man
and it probably also applies to a lay person
when he is obsessed by the five hindrances
by sensual desire or passionate lust.
The second one is ill will or anger,
the third is sloth and topper,
and then restlessness and worry,
and the fifth one is doubt.
If he is very much disturbed by these hindrances,
then he should go and see advice from a monk
who has overcome these five hindrances,
one who has attained one point in this of mind,
and sometimes for the sake of Dhamma teaching,
it is reasonable sometimes to inconvenience a monk,
unless of course the teacher monk
is meditating or something like that,
then it's not proper to disturb him,
but if he's not meditating even at night,
it is alright for another monk to go and see
the advice of a senior monk
who is able to guide him to overcome the five hindrances.
And the sixth one is he cannot grasp
or understand the object of meditation,
he's not adapted at it,
then he should go and ask for this advice.
This monk who has developed mind,
who has developed mind here is Mano Bhavaniya,
comes from the word Bhava,
Bhavaniya comes from the word Bhavana.
This word Bhavana is development,
and in the sutras here you find it is development of Mano,
development of the mind.
In some other sutras we came across Samadhi,
Samadhi Bhavana, development of Samadhi,
and when we talk about Bhavana, development,
Bhavana is usually a word used for meditation,
it means development of the mind,
and here in the sutras is either development of the Mano,
the mind, or development of Samadhi,
and it helps us, one who has developed the mind
is one who has got rid of the five hindrances,
generally that is what is understood.
Now the next sutra is quite an important sutra,
The exalted one addressed the venerable Udayan saying,
how many states are there Udayan of ever-minding?
Now when he had spoken thus the venerable Udayan was silent,
and a second and a third time the exalted one spoke in like manner,
but the venerable Udayan remained silent.
Then said the venerable Ananda to the venerable Udayan,
Udayan, venerable sir, the master addresses you,
and Udayan said, I am listening to the exalted one,
Ananda, venerable sir.
Lord, among remembers many previous existences,
that is to say one birth, two births, and so on,
remembers many previous existences in all their modes,
in all their detail.
This Lord is a state of ever-minding.
Then said the exalted one,
I knew Ananda, this foolish fellow Udayan,
did not live in tent on higher thought.
How many states of ever-minding are there, Ananda?
Lord, there are five states, what five?
Herein Lord, among aloof from sensual desires,
enters and abides in the first jhana,
in the second jhana, in the third jhana.
This state of ever-minding, Lord,
thus may become, thus may to increase,
leads to dwelling at ease here and now.
Again Lord, he concentrates on the thought of light,
fixes his mind on the thought of day,
as by day so by night, as by night so by day.
Thus with mind neither hampered nor hindered,
he makes his mind become radiant.
This state of ever-minding, Lord,
thus may become, thus may to increase,
leads to the gain of inside knowledge, Niyanadasana.
Again Lord, he considers this body
upwards from the soles of his feet,
downwards from the hair of his head,
as skin bound, as full of diverse impurities.
There are in this body the hair of the head,
the hair of the body, nails, teeth, skin,
flesh, sinews, bones, marrow, kidney,
heart, liver, blura, spleen, lungs, intestines,
mason tree, belly, of stomach, dung, bile,
phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease,
or limp, spittle, snot, synovia,
or oil of the joint, urine.
This state of ever-minding, Lord,
thus may become, thus may to increase,
leads to the ridden of passionate lust.
Again Lord, suppose he sees a body
cast away in a cemetery, one day dead,
two days dead, or three days dead,
bloated, blue-black, a mass of pus.
He compares his own body thus.
This body too is subject thus.
Thus it will come to be.
This is not past.
Or suppose he sees such a body
being eaten by crows, ravens, vultures,
dogs, jackals, vermin.
He compares his own body in like manner.
So too, a body that is a chain of bones,
with flesh and blood, sinew bound,
or fleshless, but blood-despected, sinew bound,
or without flesh or blood, sinew bound,
or but bones scattered here and there,
here a hand bone, there a foot bone,
there a leg bone, here a thigh bone,
here a hip bone, there a bank bone,
here a skull.
Should he see a body cast away in a cemetery,
the bones of which are white as a seashell,
a heap of bones, a rotting, powdering mass,
years old, he compares his own body thus.
This body too is subject thus.
Thus it will come to be.
This is not past.
This state of ever-minding, Lord,
thus made become, thus made to increase,
leads to the rooting out of the conceit I am.
Then, Lord, putting by pleasure,
among enters and abides in the fourth jhana,
this state of ever-minding, Lord,
thus made become, thus made to increase,
leads to the complete penetration
of the countless elements.
Lord, there are these five states of ever-minding.
Sādhu, sādhu, ananda,
well done, well done, ananda,
and hold to this sixth to be a state of ever-minding.
Herein, ananda, a monk goes out mindful,
comes in mindful, stands mindful, sits mindful,
lies down mindful, and is mindful in performing action.
This state of ever-minding, ananda,
thus made become, thus made to increase,
leads to mindfulness and self-possession.
That's the end of the sutta.
This is a very good sutta.
Here, there are five states of ever-minding
or mindfulness,
that, remember, ananda said,
the first one is the three jhanas,
which leads to a dwelling at ease here and now.
And the second one is,
he concentrates on the thought of light,
fixes his mind on the thought of day,
as by day, so by night, as by night, so by day.
Thus, with mind neither hampered nor hindered,
it makes his mind become radiant,
and this leads to insight knowledge.
So, you see, this is the best condition
for a person to obtain nāna-dāsana.
Seeing, sometimes it's translated as seeing and knowing,
sometimes it's translated as insight knowledge.
So, from here, you can understand the purpose of samādhi,
the purpose of jāna.
Why jāna is very often stressed in the Buddha's discourses.
And the reason is, jāna, literally,
is a state of mental incandescence
when the mind becomes very bright.
And here, we know why the Buddha wants us
to cultivate the mind very bright
because it leads to insight knowledge.
If the mind is dull, it's not bright.
It does not lead to insight knowledge.
Then the third thing that Nanda mentioned
is the meditation on the 32 parts of the body.
And here, it is said that the meditation
on the 32 parts of the body leads
to the riddance of passionate lust.
Lust is abandoned this way.
This meditation on the 32 parts of the body
is both a samādha and a vipassana practice,
a tranquilization practice,
as well as a contemplation practice.
In the earliest uttā, we read,
Anguta Nikāya 2.3.10,
they said that samādha leads to the riddance
of passionate lust.
Samādha just plain samādhi itself,
when the mind becomes very strong,
very strongly concentrated,
the hindrances are abandoned,
and passionate lust is one of the hindrances.
So this is another way to get rid of passionate lust
by contemplation on the 32 parts of the body.
Then the fourth one is meditation on the corpse,
the different types of corpse,
a corpse that is three days old,
bloated, blue-black, a mass of past, etc.
Then when we think of this type of corpse,
then we think of our own body.
One day our body will also be like that,
foul-smelling, rotting,
or few days later,
it is broken up,
eaten up by crows,
vouchers, dogs, worms, etc.
So in the same way, we also think
that one day our body will be like that,
or after some time,
when most of the flesh is eaten up,
then it is a chain of bones,
with some flesh left,
some blood left,
and the sinews are still there
and binding all the bones together.
Or it is fleshless, no more flesh,
but still some blood on it,
and the sinews are still there
and binding, holding all the bones together.
Or later, without flesh or blood,
and then later,
when the sinews are also gone
and then the bones are scattered here and there,
here a hand bone, there a foot bone,
there a leg bone, etc.
Then later, the bones become white as a seashell,
and later even turns to powder.
So when we think about how this body
is going to end up that way,
it helps us to get rid of the conceit I am.
Then the fifth one is the fourth jhana,
and it is stated here that the fourth jhana
leads to the complete penetration
of the countless elements.
The fourth jhana is superior
to the earlier three jhanas,
and because it is such a tranquil state
that deep insight can be obtained
from the fourth jhana.
And then the sixth one that the Buddha mentioned
is mindfulness of our body
in the sense that
a monk goes out mindful,
comes in mindful, stands mindful, sits mindful,
lies down mindful, and is mindful
in performing action.
This leads to mindfulness and self-possession.
By itself, it doesn't give you insight
when you are mindful of your bodily actions.
By itself, it does not lead us to much wisdom,
but it maintains our mindfulness
and self-possession.
Sati sampajanya.
And if we can maintain our mindfulness
without it being broken,
then it leads to concentration,
to a low stage of concentration.
And it helps us to develop samadhi.
In the Satipathana Sutra,
there are various contemplations on the body,
and the earlier ones are more useful,
like the 32 parts of the body,
the corpse and all that,
because especially the corpse one,
it gives us some urgency to practice.
Let's stop here for a while.
The next sutra is 6.3.30
Mounds, these six are above all.
What six? The sight above all,
the sound above all, the gain above all,
the training above all,
the service above all,
the ever-minding above all.
And what mounds is the sight above all?
Here in mounds, some go to see the treasures,
the elephant, the horse, the gem,
to see the diverse things,
to see some recluse or Brahmin wrong in view,
treading the wrong path.
And is that the sight mounds?
No, I say it is not.
It is indeed a mean sight,
common, vulgar, un-Aryan,
not well-founded,
leading not to world-wearingness,
dispassion, ending, calm,
knowledge, awakening, nibbana.
But some, endowed with faith and piety,
sure in trust, go to see the Tathagata,
or the Tathagata's disciple.
That mounds is the sight above all sights,
for purging man, overcoming grief and woe,
clearing away ill and pain,
winning truth, and realizing nibbana.
I mean going to see the Tathagata or his disciple,
endowed with faith and piety,
sure in trust.
This mounds is called the sight above all.
Herein is the sight above all.
And what mounds is the sound above all?
Herein mounds, some go to hear the sound of the drum,
the lute, the sound of song,
to hear diverse things,
to hear the dhamma of some recluse or Brahmin,
wrong in view, treading the wrong path.
And is that the sound mounds?
No, I say it is not.
It is indeed a mean sound,
common, vulgar, un-Aryan, etc.
But some, endowed with faith and piety,
sure in trust, go to hear the Tathagata's dhamma
or his disciples.
That mounds is the sound above all sounds
for purging man.
I mean going to hear the Tathagata's dhamma
or his disciples' dhamma or his disciples.
This mounds is called the sound above all.
Herein is the sight and sound above all.
And what mounds is the gain above all?
Herein mounds, some gain the gain of a son
or a wife or wealth,
gain diverse things,
gain faith in some recluse or Brahmin,
wrong in view, treading the wrong path.
And is that the gain mounds?
No, I say it is not.
It is indeed a mean gain.
But some, endowed with faith and piety,
sure in trust,
gain faith in the Tathagata or his disciples.
That mounds is the gain above all gains
for purging man.
I mean gaining faith in the Tathagata or his disciples.
This mounds is called the gain above all.
Herein is the sight, sound and gain above all.
And what mounds is the training above all?
Herein mounds, some train in elephant law,
in horsemanship,
in the use of the chariot, the bow, the sword,
train in diverse ways,
train after the way of some recluse or Brahmin,
wrong in view, treading the wrong path.
And is that the training mounds?
No, I say it is not.
It is indeed a mean training.
But some, endowed with faith and piety,
sure in trust,
train in the onward course of higher virtue,
higher mind and higher wisdom,
according to the Dhamma Vinaya
declared by the Tathagata.
That mounds is the training above all training
for purging man.
I mean training in the onward course of higher virtue,
higher mind and higher wisdom.
This mounds is called the training above all.
Herein is the sight, sound, gain and training above all.
And what mounds is the service above all?
Herein mounds, some serve a warrior, a Brahmin,
a householder, serve diverse folks,
serve some recluse or Brahmin,
wrong in view, treading the wrong path.
And is that the service mounds?
No, I say it is not.
It is indeed a mean service.
But some, endowed with faith and piety,
sure in trust,
serve the Tathagata or his disciple.
That mounds is the service above all services
for purging man.
I mean serving the Tathagata or his disciple.
This mounds is called the service above all.
Herein is the sight, sound,
gain, training and service above all.
And what mounds is the ever-minding above all?
Herein mounds, some ever-mind the gain
of a son or a wife or wealth,
ever-mind diverse gains,
ever-mind some recluse or Brahmin,
wrong in view, treading the wrong path.
And is that the ever-minding mounds?
No, I say it is not.
It is indeed a mean ever-minding,
common, vulgar, un-Aryan,
not well-founded,
leading not to world-wearingness, dispassion,
ending calm, knowledge, awakening Nibbana.
But some endowed with faith and piety,
sure in trust, ever-mind the Tathagata
or the Tathagata's disciple.
That mounds is the ever-minding above all,
ever-minding for purging man.
Overcoming grief and war,
clearing away ill and pain,
winning truth and realizing Nibbana.
I mean ever-minding the Tathagata or his disciple,
endowed with faith and piety, sure in trust.
This mounds is called the ever-minding above all.
Verily mounds, these are the six above all.
That's the end of the Sutta.
Here the Buddha is saying that the
the sight above all sights
is going to see the Tathagata or his disciple.
And then the sound above all sounds
is to hear the Tathagata's Dhamma
or his disciples.
And the gain above all gains
is to gain faith in the Tathagata or his disciple.
And the training above all
is the training in the higher virtue,
the higher mind, and the higher wisdom.
It's the Adi Sila, Adi Chitta, and Adi Panya.
And then the service above all
is to serve the Tathagata or his disciple.
And the ever-minding above all
that is remembering
is to ever-mind the Tathagata
or the Tathagata's disciple.
You see, the Buddha is saying
the right path leads to
world-weariness, dispassion, ending, calm,
knowledge, awakening, nibbana.
If we practice anything that leads to
greater passion for the world,
greater excitement for the world,
that is not the spiritual path,
not the holy path.
It is a path that leads to
enjoyment of the world,
which ends in moduka.
But the path that leads to
weariness of the world
is a path that leads to dispassion
for the world.
And that is a path that leads
ultimately to the greatest happiness.
